title: American vs. Chinese vs. Riichi Mahjong: Which Should You Play?
description: The three main styles of mahjong — American (NMJL), Chinese, and Japanese Riichi — are almost different games. Here's how they compare and how to pick.
author: MahjNearMe
body:
Walk into any mahjong group in the United States and the first question you'll be asked is: "What style do you play?" The answer matters. American, Chinese, and Japanese Riichi mahjong share the same beautiful tiles, but they're played with wildly different rules, rhythms, and cultures. Pick the wrong one and you'll spend your first night politely confused.

Here's a clear-eyed comparison to help you decide.

The short answer

  • American Mahjong (NMJL) — most popular in the U.S., especially in Jewish community centers, senior centers, and country clubs. Uses a hand card that changes every year.
  • Chinese Mahjong — the oldest and most internationally played style. More intuitive once you learn the basics, with fewer memorized hands.
  • Japanese Riichi Mahjong — the style you see in anime and on streaming platforms. The most strategic of the three. Beloved by younger players.

American Mahjong (NMJL)

American Mahjong is a 20th-century invention, played almost exclusively in the United States. The National Mah Jongg League (NMJL) publishes a new card every April with roughly 70 legal hands. To win, you need to build one of those exact hands.

Who plays it: Players of all ages, but historically dominated by women over 50, with strong roots in Jewish-American communities. Increasingly popular with Gen Z players who've discovered it through TikTok.

Pace: Games are typically 3–4 hours long. Eight to ten hands per session. Conversation and socializing are part of the game.

What to expect: You'll be juggling the NMJL card, counting jokers, and calling "Mahjong!" when you complete a hand. You'll also pick up a lot of tradition — some groups have specific rituals for picking jokers, throwing tiles, and declaring hands.

Where to find it: JCCs, senior centers, country clubs, and a growing number of young mahjong clubs in major cities. Search MahjNearMe for "American" or "NMJL" in your area.

Chinese Mahjong (Hong Kong / Taiwanese / Mainland)

Chinese Mahjong is the original. It came out of 19th-century Qing Dynasty China and branched into regional styles: Hong Kong (the most common internationally), Taiwanese (16-tile hands instead of 13), and various mainland variants.

Who plays it: Chinese-American communities, international students, and an increasing number of younger American players drawn to the simplicity.

Pace: Faster than American — hands can end in just a few rounds. A full session is typically 2–3 hours.

What to expect: You don't need a card. Winning hands are based on patterns (pongs, kongs, chows, and a pair) plus a simpler scoring system. The rules feel more like a card game once you get them.

Where to find it: Chinese community centers, universities with large international populations, and a growing number of Asian American social clubs.

Japanese Riichi Mahjong

Riichi is the style featured in anime like Akagi and Saki, and the style played competitively in Japan. It emerged in the 20th century from Chinese roots but developed into the most structured and mathematically deep variant.

Who plays it: Younger players, chess-and-poker-adjacent strategy enthusiasts, and the online Mahjong Soul community.

Pace: Can be fast (east-only games run ~30 minutes) or long (east-south matches are closer to 90 minutes).

What to expect: Deep strategy. Riichi has concepts like "reading" opponents from their discards, defensive play, and yaku (scoring patterns) that take time to learn. The learning curve is steeper, but the depth is closer to poker than to a casual card game.

Where to find it: University anime clubs, Discord communities, and Mahjong Soul online. In-person Riichi clubs exist in most major U.S. cities but you'll have to look harder — check our search page.

Which one should you start with?

  • If you have family who plays American Mahjong — start there. You'll have free lessons and a built-in group.
  • If you want the simplest on-ramp — Chinese Mahjong. Fewer memorized hands, more pattern recognition, and the rules scale from casual to competitive gracefully.
  • If you love strategy games (chess, poker, Go) — Riichi. It's the deepest of the three and has the most active online scene.
  • If you want to meet people in your neighborhood — whichever style your local group plays. Community beats preference.

Can you play all three?

Yes. Most serious mahjong players eventually try multiple styles. The tiles are the same, the calling ritual is similar, and after you know one style the others take a fraction of the time to learn.

The trick is not mixing them up mid-game. Every mahjong night operates under one ruleset.

Ready to find a game?

Search MahjNearMe for groups in your area — you can filter by game style to see only American, Chinese, or Riichi listings. If you're brand-new, our instructors directory is the fastest path to a first lesson.
